mappings.rationale: Directly addresses the nature and scope of literature, literary genres, and critical-theoretical writing about literature (A.1, A.7).
Covers rhetoric as the art of discourse, including figures of speech, oratory, and prose style (B.1).
Directly covers poetry as a literary form, including verse/prose distinctions and lyric traditions (C.1, C.4).
Covers American poetic traditions including lyric and transnational poetry, relevant to C.1 and C.4.
Directly covers epic as narrative imaginative literature including literary, beast, mock, and romantic epic (C.2.a).
Directly covers the short story and its antecedents as a literary form (C.2.e).
Covers fairy tales as a literary form, directly relevant to fable, parable, allegory, and folk literature (C.2.f, C.8).
Covers the contemporary novel and its genres including historical fiction and postmodernism (C.2.d).
Covers crime fiction as a popular literary genre spanning the novel and short story (C.2.d, C.2.e, C.8).
Covers horror as a literary genre across fiction, drama, and popular literature (C.2, C.8).
Covers science fiction as a literary genre of narrative imaginative literature (C.2).
Covers Gothic romance and Gothic literary traditions, relevant to romance and narrative fiction (C.2.c).
Directly covers tragedy as a dramatic literary form (C.3.a).
Directly covers comedy as a dramatic literary form, including pantomime, parody, and Restoration comedy (C.3.b).
Covers modern dramatic literature including naturalism, realism, and symbolism in theatre texts (C.3).
Covers autobiographical literature including diary, journal, and memoir forms (C.6.o).
Covers biographical literature including critical, popular, and interpretive biography (C.6.o).
Directly covers children's literature as a literary category (C.7).
Covers popular literature and the relationship between literature and its audience (A.4, C.8).
Addresses the comparative study of literary works across languages and traditions, relevant to literary scholarship (A.7).
Covers interpretation theory as applied to literary texts, relevant to critical writing about literature (A.7).
A major approach to literary criticism addressing language, identity, and textual meaning (A.7).
Barthes is a foundational literary critic and theorist whose work on text, sign, and criticism is central to writings about literature (A.7).
Derrida's deconstruction is a major critical-theoretical approach to literary texts (A.7).
Myth is a foundational source for epic, saga, fable, and allegory, all explicitly listed as literary forms (C.2).
Directly addresses imagination in literary theory and the creative process of literary composition (A.2, A.3).
Covers the relationship between literature and its audience, including literacy and reading practices (A.4).
Covers the history of writing systems, relevant to the distinction between literature and other forms of writing (A.1).
Covers literary translation and meaning across languages, relevant to literature's audience and comparative study (A.4, A.7).
Covers lexicography and the history of word usage, relevant to literary diction, style, and the tools of composition (B.1.b).
Covers ancient Greek and Roman literature including epic, tragedy, comedy, lyric, and satire (C, D.1).
Covers medieval literary forms including romance, courtly love poetry, and religious drama (C.2.c, C.3.f, D.1).
Covers Romantic literature spanning poetry, the novel, and drama as a major Western literary movement (D.1).
Covers literary modernism as a major movement transforming the novel, poetry, and drama in Western literature (D.1).
Covers the Decadent literary movement as a significant phase in Western literary history (D.1).
Covers the Beat literary movement in American poetry and the novel, relevant to Western literary history (D.1).
Covers a major Western literary tradition from Chaucer to the present (D.1).
Covers a major Western literary tradition from the Middle Ages to the nouveau roman (D.1).
Covers a major Western literary tradition including Sturm und Drang and modern movements (D.1).
Covers a major Western literary tradition from the Middle Ages through modern Italy (D.1).
Covers a major Western literary tradition including Golden Age drama and the modern novel (D.1).
Covers a major Western literary tradition from prose to socialist realism (D.1).
Covers a major non-Western literary tradition including poetry, fiction, and vernacular writing (D).
Covers a major non-Western literary tradition including poetry, fiction, and The Tale of Genji (D).
Covers Latin American literary traditions blending indigenous and European forms (D).
Covers modern Latin American fiction and poetry including magical realism (D).
Covers a distinctive literary tradition relevant to primitive, folk, and popular literature (C.8).
Covers a literary tradition rooted in doctrinal and religious writing with global diaspora dimensions (C.6.d, D).
Addresses religious scripture as literature, directly relevant to doctrinal and religious literature (C.6.d).
Homer's Iliad and Odyssey are foundational epics, directly relevant to epic as narrative literature (C.2.a).
Ovid's works span epic, lyric, and narrative poetry across multiple literary genres (C.1, C.2, C.4).
Horace's odes, satires, and epistles exemplify lyric, satiric, and epistolary literature (C.4, C.5, C.6.m).
Sophocles is a foundational tragedian, directly relevant to tragedy as dramatic literature (C.3.a).
Dante's works span epic, allegory, and lyric poetry, all explicitly listed as literary forms (C.2.a, C.2.f, C.4).
Chaucer's works span narrative poetry, romance, and fable as well as medieval literary forms (C.2.c, C.2.f).
Shakespeare's works span tragedy, comedy, tragicomedy, and lyric poetry, all listed as literary forms (C.3, C.4).
Directly covers Shakespearean comedy as dramatic literature (C.3.b).
Directly covers Shakespearean tragedy as dramatic literature (C.3.a).
Covers Shakespeare's sonnets and lyric poetry, directly relevant to language-based lyrics (C.4.b).
Goethe's works span drama, lyric poetry, and the novel, major forms covered in sections C.2, C.3, and C.4.
Austen is a major novelist whose works exemplify the novel and satiric prose (C.2.d, C.5).
Dickens is a major novelist and social satirist, relevant to the novel and satiric literature (C.2.d, C.5).
Tolstoy is a major novelist, relevant to the novel and philosophical literature (C.2.d, C.6.e).
Dostoevsky is a major novelist whose works engage philosophy, ethics, and religion (C.2.d, C.6.e).
Joyce is a major modernist novelist whose experimental forms transformed the novel (C.2.d, D.1).
Proust is a major modernist novelist whose work explores memory and narrative form (C.2.d, D.1).
Kafka wrote novels, short stories, and parables, relevant to multiple narrative forms (C.2.d, C.2.e, C.2.f).
Trollope is a major Victorian novelist whose prolific output in political and social fiction exemplifies the novel as narrative imaginative literature (C.2.d).
Eliot is a major realist novelist, relevant to the novel as narrative imaginative literature (C.2.d).
James is a major novelist and literary critic, relevant to the novel and criticism as literature (C.2.d, C.6.c).
Melville's Moby-Dick and other novels span epic, allegorical, and philosophical fiction (C.2.a, C.2.d, C.2.f).
Shelley's Frankenstein bridges Gothic romance and science fiction, relevant to romance and narrative fiction (C.2.c, C.2.d).
Zola is the leading naturalist novelist, relevant to the novel and Western literary history (C.2.d, D.1).
Borges's short fiction and use of allegory and parable are relevant to the short story and related forms (C.2.e, C.2.f).
Tolkien's fantasy draws on epic and romance traditions, and his works are major children's and popular literature (C.2.a, C.2.c, C.7).
Christie is a major crime fiction author, relevant to the novel and popular literature (C.2.d, C.8).
Montaigne founded the essay as a literary form, directly relevant to nonfictional prose (C.6.a).
Thoreau's works span the essay, travel literature, and philosophical literature as nonfictional prose (C.6.a, C.6.e, C.6.l).
Voltaire wrote satire, philosophical tales, and polemical literature, all listed as literary forms (C.5, C.6.e, C.6.g).
Shaw is a major playwright and essayist, relevant to dramatic literature and the essay (C.3, C.6.a).
Plath is a major confessional poet, relevant to lyric poetry and autobiographical literature (C.4, C.6.o).
Bishop is a major lyric poet whose travel poetry also serves as travel literature (C.4, C.6.l).
Camus wrote novels, plays, and philosophical essays across multiple literary forms (C.2.d, C.3, C.6.e).
Lewis wrote fantasy, children's literature, and theological prose, spanning multiple listed forms (C.2.c, C.6.d, C.7).
Postcolonial literary criticism is a major approach to writings about literature and non-Western traditions (A.7).
Covers theories of comedy, farce, satire, and parody as literary and performative forms (C.3.b, C.3.d, C.5).
A controversial figure in literary history whose works challenged Enlightenment morality and influenced the novel of transgression, relevant to C.2.d and D.4.b.
A pioneering writer and political thinker whose Vindication of the Rights of Woman combined literary and philosophical argument, relevant to C.3.b (essay) and D.4.b.
Examines the literary genres, narrative techniques, and rhetorical strategies of the New Testament texts, relevant to C.1 (epic and narrative forms) and C.3 (nonfiction prose).
